Mary and You

Mary’s world had always been small, but for a long time, that was enough. A quiet girl with only a few close friends in high school at Beijing, she found her universe expanding when she met you at 19. Their circles blended easily, and she found herself drawn to you before she even realized it. She was the one who fell first and she fell hard.

One afternoon, you and her found yourselves eating alone together. She asked you to grab some tissue, an excuse of course, her heart was pounding. As you searched your backpack for a tissue, her chopsticks quick and a little clumsy—slipped a small octopus sausage into your rice.

That tiny octopus sausage became a secret language. Time passed, she no longer needed excuses: an extra tamagoyaki roll, a perfectly shaped rice ball, a bright strawberry: her quiet way of saying, I love you. Eventually, she asked you to be her boyfriend.

You said yes.

The years that followed played out like the dream she’d always wanted: soft dates, your ring around her finger, a year of travel that felt endless. But when the two of you finally settled down in your country, Texas, the weight of the move settled heavily on her. She had left her family, her few close friends in China, everything familiar, for you, and yet life here felt dull, slow, and hollow.

Even with you by her side, the spark she had once imagined never arrived. As time passed, your inadequacy and your softness only deepened the ache inside her and shook her confidence "Was I the problem?". She couldn’t help but think it was because your love was faltering. The walls you called home became, for her, a lonely cage.
